Femur.name: Femur
Femur.function: The thigh bone — the longest, strongest bone in the body. Its rounded lower end (the condyles) forms the top half of the knee joint and rolls and glides on the tibia as you bend and straighten your leg.

Tibia.name: Tibia (shin bone)
Tibia.function: The shin bone, the larger of the two lower-leg bones. Its broad, flat top (the tibial plateau) forms the bottom half of the knee joint and carries almost all of your body weight down to the ankle.

Fibula.name: Fibula
Fibula.function: The slender bone running alongside the tibia on the outer side of the lower leg. It bears little weight but anchors muscles and ligaments and steadies the outer edge of the knee and ankle.

Patella.name: Patella (kneecap)
Patella.function: The kneecap — a small bone set inside the tendon of the thigh muscle. It glides in a groove at the front of the femur and acts like a pulley, giving the quadriceps extra leverage to straighten the knee.

Anterior cruciate ligament.name: Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL)
Anterior cruciate ligament.function: A strong band deep inside the joint running from the back of the femur to the front of the tibia. It stops the shin from sliding forward and gives the knee its rotational stability — the ligament most often torn in sport.

Posterior cruciate ligament.name: Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL)
Posterior cruciate ligament.function: The thicker of the two crossing ligaments in the centre of the knee. It runs from the front of the femur to the back of the tibia and stops the shin from sliding backwards, keeping the joint steady when you kneel or land.

Lateral meniscus.name: Lateral Meniscus
Lateral meniscus.function: A C-shaped wedge of tough cartilage on the outer side of the knee, sitting between the femur and tibia. It cushions and spreads the load, absorbs shock and helps the rounded bone ends fit smoothly together.

Medial meniscus.name: Medial Meniscus
Medial meniscus.function: A crescent of cartilage on the inner side of the knee that acts as a shock absorber and spacer between the femur and tibia. It is more firmly attached than its partner, which makes it more prone to tearing with a twist.
